Job contrasts his present condition with his former honor and authority. What little reason do people have to be ambitious or proud of something that can be so easily lost, and what little confidence should be put in it! We should not be discouraged if we are despised, reviled, and hated by wicked men. We should look to Jesus, who endured the contradiction of sinners.

Job complains a great deal. Harboring hard thoughts of God was the sin which at this time most easily beset Job. When inward temptations join with outward calamities, the soul is hurried as in a tempest and is filled with confusion. But woe be to those who really have God for an enemy! Compared with the awful state of ungodly men, what are all outward or even inward temporal afflictions? There is something with which Job comforts himself, yet it is but a little. He foresees that death will be the end of all his troubles. God’s wrath might bring him to death; but his soul would be safe and happy in the world of spirits. If none pity us, yet our God, who corrects, pities us, even as a father pities his own children. And let us look more to the things of eternity: then the believer will cease from mourning and joyfully praise redeeming love.
